skill.name: vercel
skill.description: Deploy and manage Vercel projects, production releases, domains, environment variables, teams, and local development using the Vercel CLI.
skill.summary: Use the Vercel CLI to manage deployments, projects, domains, environment variables, teams, and local development.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby backtick and weak cryptography patterns, but the reviewed file is Markdown documentation for Vercel CLI commands, not executable Ruby code. Real risk remains medium because the skill instructs external CLI execution, includes destructive project and environment variable commands, and documents pulling secrets into a local .env file.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Environment Data Can Be Written Locally
medium_findings.description: The skill documents pulling Vercel environment variables into a local .env file. This is legitimate Vercel workflow support, but it can expose secrets in the workspace if used without care. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for sensitive file access. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: SKILL.md explicitly says to pull env vars to a local .env file and shows the matching Vercel command, so the sensitive-data behavior is clear but not malicious.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md explicitly says to pull env vars to a local .env file and shows the matching Vercel command. The action is sensitive but also a normal Vercel workflow, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

medium_findings.title: Operationally Destructive Vercel Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes commands for production deployment, project removal, environment variable removal, and account logout. These commands can alter live infrastructure or remove configuration if run against the wrong project. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE semantic risk. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The commands and their stated purposes are visible in SKILL.md, but the skill is a reference guide and does not force execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and their purposes are present in the file. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the skill documents standard Vercel CLI actions.

low_findings.title: Markdown CLI Examples Misclassified As Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: Static analysis identified many Ruby or shell backtick execution patterns, including inline Markdown code spans and fenced bash examples. The file contains documentation for Vercel CLI commands, not executable Ruby code. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for Ruby backtick execution. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The reviewed file is SKILL.md and the flagged syntax appears inside Markdown prose or bash fences rather than Ruby source.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax appears in Markdown prose and fenced bash examples, not in Ruby source. The examples still represent external command guidance, so only the Ruby-specific interpretation is dismissed.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Supported By Evidence
low_findings.description: Static analysis marked line 3 as weak cryptography, but that line is the skill description mentioning the Vercel CLI. No cryptographic algorithm is present. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.97. Confidence reasoning: The cited line contains only descriptive metadata and no hash, cipher, or cryptographic operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains only descriptive metadata. No cryptographic algorithm or operation is present at that location.

low_findings.title: Account Identity Check Is Benign Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged the whoami command as system reconnaissance. In context, it checks the active Vercel login before performing account actions and does not enumerate host system details. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious reconnaissance. Confidence: 0.9. Confidence reasoning: The line is under the Authentication section and uses the Vercel CLI to identify the current Vercel account.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is scoped to Vercel account authentication. It does not collect host, network, or credential inventory beyond the logged-in account identity.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Vercel CLI Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is built around running Vercel CLI commands. This is expected for the skill purpose, but commands can deploy code, modify domains, remove resources, and write environment variables locally. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: The file repeatedly documents direct Vercel CLI invocations across deployment, project, domain, environment, team, and authentication workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file repeatedly documents direct Vercel CLI invocations across multiple operational workflows. The pattern is intentional and legitimate, but it carries infrastructure risk.

content.value_statement: Vercel operations often require many CLI commands across deployments, domains, teams, and environment variables. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concise Vercel CLI reference for common project tasks.
actual_capabilities: Shows commands for preview and production deployments with the Vercel CLI.
Lists commands for inspecting deployments and viewing deployment logs.
Provides commands for linking, listing, and removing Vercel projects.
Covers domain listing, domain addition, and domain inspection workflows.
Documents environment variable listing, addition, removal, and local pulling.
Includes local development, build, team switching, login, and logout commands.
limitations: Does not validate project names, domains, or deployment URLs before commands run.
Does not provide rollback policy, approval gates, or production safety checks.
Requires the Vercel CLI to be installed and authenticated in the user environment.
May expose secrets if environment variables are pulled into an unprotected workspace.

use_cases.title: Release a Web Application
use_cases.description: Find the correct Vercel CLI commands for preview deployments, production deployments, deployment inspection, and log review.

use_cases.title: Configure Project Operations
use_cases.description: Manage Vercel project links, domains, environment variables, local builds, and team context from a single command reference.

use_cases.title: Support Account And Team Tasks
use_cases.description: Check the current Vercel login, switch teams, list teams, and guide collaborators through common CLI account actions.

prompt_templates.title: List Deployment Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the Vercel CLI commands for deploying the current project, deploying to production, and listing recent deployments.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect A Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me inspect a Vercel deployment and view its logs. Ask for the deployment URL before giving the commands.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Environment Variables
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through listing, adding, pulling, and removing Vercel environment variables. Include cautions about local .env files.

prompt_templates.title: Plan A Production Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe Vercel CLI workflow for linking a project, checking the active team, validating domains, building locally, and deploying to production.

output_examples.input: I need to deploy my current project to Vercel production.
output_examples.output: The assistant would confirm the intended project and team, then provide the production deployment command and suggest inspecting the deployment after it completes.

output_examples.input: I need to work with Vercel environment variables.
output_examples.output: The assistant would list the relevant Vercel CLI commands and warn that pulling variables writes sensitive values to a local environment file.

output_examples.input: I need to add a custom domain to a Vercel project.
output_examples.output: The assistant would ask for the domain and project name, then provide the domain add and domain inspect commands.

best_practices: Confirm the active Vercel team and project before running deploy, remove, or environment commands.
Treat pulled environment files as secrets and keep them out of source control.
Run local builds or preview deployments before production deployments when possible.
anti_patterns: Do not run production deployment commands without confirming the target project and team.
Do not paste secrets into prompts, logs, issue trackers, or shared chat history.
Do not remove projects or environment variables without a backup or explicit approval.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides Vercel CLI command guidance. The user or agent environment must run the commands.

faq.question: Does it require the Vercel CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflows depend on the Vercel CLI being installed and authenticated.

faq.question: Can it manage production deployments?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes the production deployment command, but users should confirm project and team context first.

faq.question: Can it work with environment variables?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers listing, adding, pulling, and removing Vercel environment variables.

faq.question: Is pulling environment variables safe?
faq.answer: It can be safe when handled carefully. The local .env file may contain secrets and must be protected.

faq.question: Does this skill include Vercel API integration?
faq.answer: No. The skill only documents Vercel CLI workflows and does not include API code.
